**Background.** Large language models (LLMs) have undergone rapid transformation in clinical research, evolving from simple prompt-based query tools to sophisticated agentic systems capable of orchestrating multi-step reasoning across complex biomedical workflows. This evolution has created both unprecedented opportunities for accelerating research and critical questions around safety, validation, and equitable deployment.

**Objective.** We conducted a systematic review to characterize the breadth and depth of LLM applications across the clinical research pipeline, from prompt engineering and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to agentic coding interfaces and autonomous clinical decision support.

**Methods.** A systematic search of Scopus, PubMed, and Embase covering 2024 through 2026 identified 1,617 records; after deduplication, 1,063 articles were screened, and 55 studies from 47 journals met inclusion criteria. Studies were eligible if they evaluated LLM performance in a clinical research context with quantitative outcomes.

**Results.** Across the included studies, LLMs demonstrated substantial but heterogeneous performance. In structured information extraction tasks, GPT-4o achieved F1 scores of 94.0% for medication extraction and 78.1% for discontinuation classification from EHR notes, with open-source models such as Llama-3.1-70B performing comparably in privacy-sensitive settings [@Shao2026Scalable]. Prompt engineering meaningfully modulated accuracy: optimized GPT-4o prompts for breast cancer nomogram inputs achieved intraclass correlation coefficients of 0.95 versus 0.57--0.62 for unoptimized prompts [@Shah2026Integrating], while GPT-4 extracted colorectal cancer TNM staging with 89--96% accuracy across individual stage components [@Kim2025Using]. Hybrid neuro-symbolic approaches combining LLMs with rule-based expert systems achieved F1 scores of 1.00 for study inclusion classification, compared with a GPT-4 standalone F1 of 0.63 [@Prenosil2025Neurosymbolic]. In clinical decision support, LLMs surpassed 1,106 emergency physicians on NSTEMI knowledge questions (9/10 vs. mean 7.62 ± 1.32 correct; *p* < 0.05) [@Yorgancolu2025Performance], yet zero-shot performance on ICD-9 medical coding remained modest across all tested models, with the best-performing LLaMA-3.1 achieving only 42.6% correctness [@Naliyatthaliyazchayil2025Evaluating]. EHR-integrated LLMs reduced the proportion of hospital course summaries requiring resident editing (31.5% vs. 44.8%; *p* < 0.001) but introduced significantly more confabulations (*p* = 0.002) [@Small2025Evaluating]. Pooled accuracy for patient-facing LLM responses reached 81.87% (95% CI: 77.24%--86.51%), with 69.9% rated clinically acceptable [@Zhang2025Leveraging]. Ambient AI systems deployed within a pragmatic clinical trial achieved a weighted median note-completion utilization of 65.4%, though ICD-10 documentation accuracy transiently dropped from 79% to 35% following a workflow change, recovering only after template redesign and retraining [@Afshar2025A].

**Conclusions.** LLMs are transitioning from passive information retrieval tools to active partners in clinical research, demonstrating measurable gains in documentation efficiency, structured data extraction, and decision support benchmarking. However, persistent challenges in confabulation, task-specific performance variability, and the absence of prospective clinical validation must be addressed before these systems can be responsibly embedded in high-stakes research workflows. Future work should prioritize human-LLM collaborative frameworks, rigorous real-world evaluation paradigms, and transparency mechanisms that preserve clinical accountability.
